title: Hello
pedagogy: Vocabulary: สวัสดี (sà-wàt-dii: high+dead→low, ส-led cluster high+dead→low, mid+live→mid) and the polite particles ครับ (low+dead-short→high), ค่ะ (low+mai ek→falling, statements), คะ (low+dead-short→high, questions). Tone contrast ค่ะ/คะ drilled as a minimal pair.

exercises.title: One word opens every door
body: สวัสดี works for hello AND goodbye, morning and midnight — one greeting for everything.
It usually comes with a wai: palms together, a small bow. Return one when offered; you don't wai children or staff serving you.
You can already read this word — and its tones follow the rules you know.

exercises.title: ครับ and ค่ะ — the politeness switches
body: Thai doesn't say “please” much. Politeness rides on one small particle at the end of the sentence.
Men end with ครับ (kráp). Women end with ค่ะ (khâ) for statements — and คะ (khá) for questions.
Same syllable, different tone, different job. Your ear is about to learn the difference.

exercises.context: You step into a small pharmacy in Chiang Mai. You're a man, greeting the pharmacist.
exercises.question: What do you say?

choices.thai: สวัสดีครับ

exercises.explanation: The particle follows the speaker: a man says ครับ, whoever he's talking to. ค่ะ and คะ are for female speakers — and คะ only ends questions.

exercises.context: You're a woman checking in at a guesthouse, greeting the owner.
exercises.question: What do you say?

choices.thai: สวัสดีครับ

exercises.explanation: A greeting is a statement, so a woman ends it with falling-tone ค่ะ. High-tone คะ would make it sound like a question, and ครับ is for male speakers.
